title: Presigned Browser Uploads, POST Policies, and CORS · Language Lens: Direct Upload Finalization
summary: A browser receives a broad week-long upload URL, changes signed headers, overwrites a shared key, and mistakes CORS success for storage authorization. Predict the representation, storage, trust, cost, and recovery evidence before inspecting a model. Planned artifact: Add and verify the direct upload finalization increment in least-privilege direct-upload session contract. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Finalize by matching intent to HEAD or event evidence, checksum, version, size, owner, scan result, and idempotent state transition.
Configure the smallest origin, method, header, exposure, and age CORS rules while keeping IAM and bucket policy as authorization.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: S3, CloudFront, and Go Predict Direct Upload Finalization · Theory Direct Upload Finalization
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory Go file-delivery team handles case 6757 during a compliance restore drill. Build deterministic Go evidence to finalize a direct upload against intent while separating CORS from IAM authorization; record cleanup, rollback, restoration, and residual-risk ownership; then introduce a duplicate event or ambiguous response and reconcile without duplicate effect. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, databases, credentials, host state, race and leak checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Direct Upload Finalization contract for the supplied data.

Evidence target: S3, CloudFront, and Go Predict Direct Upload Finalization · Theory Direct Upload Finalization.
